title: Failure of compromise
description: Test your skills on the events that led to the Civil War!

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b0b988a7a6135b516470dc66ede7c8d.png)

>-Source: J.L. McGee, [Library of Congress,](http://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2008661578/) 1856

**The image most directly reflects which of the following developments in the political climate in the United States during the 1850s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a growing ideological divide between pro-slavery and antislavery advocates over the extension of slavery
a decline in public support for the Democratic Party after several pieces of unpopular legislation
a significant rise in membership for the Free-Soil Party after the *Dred Scott* decision
a series of political compromises that reduced tension between antislavery and pro-slavery groups

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b0b988a7a6135b516470dc66ede7c8d.png)

>-Source: J.L. McGee, [Library of Congress,](http://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2008661578/) 1856

**Which of the following most directly led to the circumstances illustrated by the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the conflict in Kansas over the extension of slavery
the Supreme Court ruling in *Dred Scott v. Sandford*
the publication of *Uncle Tom’s Cabin*
the election of Abraham Lincoln to the presidency

assessment_items.question_content:
>“A free negro of the African race, whose ancestors were brought to this country and sold as slaves, is not a "citizen" within the meaning of the Constitution of the United States.
> “When the Constitution was adopted, they were not regarded in any of the States as members of the community which constituted the State, and were not numbered among its ‘people or citizens.’ Consequently, the special rights and immunities guaranteed to citizens do not apply to them. And not being ‘citizens’ within the meaning of the Constitution, they are not entitled to sue in that character in a court of the United States . . .”

>-Source: Chief Justice Roger B. Taney, the opinion of the Court in *Dred Scott v. Sandford,* 1857

**Which of the following was the most immediate result of the ruling excerpted?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: increased tension between abolitionists and defenders of slavery
swift resolution of the legality of slavery in western territories
widespread acceptance of the doctrine of popular sovereignty
increased support for the ratification of the Fourteenth Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
>“A free negro of the African race, whose ancestors were brought to this country and sold as slaves, is not a "citizen" within the meaning of the Constitution of the United States.
> “When the Constitution was adopted, they were not regarded in any of the States as members of the community which constituted the State, and were not numbered among its ‘people or citizens.’ Consequently, the special rights and immunities guaranteed to citizens do not apply to them. And not being ‘citizens’ within the meaning of the Constitution, they are not entitled to sue in that character in a court of the United States . . .”

>-Source: Chief Justice Roger B. Taney, the opinion of the Court in *Dred Scott v. Sandford,* 1857

**The restrictions imposed by the Dred Scott decision most directly contradicted which of the following earlier developments in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the abolition of slavery by northern state governments in the years following the American Revolution
the extension of citizenship rights to African Americans in the Fifteenth Amendment
the development of an “Underground Railroad” to help enslaved African Americans find freedom in the North
the system for counting enslaved people for purposes of representation in Congress under the Three-Fifths Compromise

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Dear Sir: Another election in Kansas Territory has passed, and, like the first, as controlled entirely by Missourians. A few days before the election, I was traveling in the southern and eastern part of the Territory, and met hundreds of people from Missouri on their way to the different voting precincts in the Territory. . . .

>“If they give us occasion to settle the question of slavery in this country with the bayonet let us improve it. What way can bring the slaves redemption more speedily. Wouldn’t it be rich to march an army through the slave holding states & roll up a black cloud that should spread dismay & terror to the ranks of the oppressors?”

>-Source: Doctor Charles Robinson, in a letter to Eli Thayer, 1855


**Which of the following most likely helped to prompt the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: efforts by national leaders and the Court to resolve the issue of slavery
armed conflicts between white settlers and Mexican-Americans in Kansas
elections of Republican candidates as representatives of Kansas in Congress
enforcement of the Fugitive Slave Law as part of the Compromise of 1850

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Dear Sir: Another election in Kansas Territory has passed, and, like the first, as controlled entirely by Missourians. A few days before the election, I was traveling in the southern and eastern part of the Territory, and met hundreds of people from Missouri on their way to the different voting precincts in the Territory. . . .

>“If they give us occasion to settle the question of slavery in this country with the bayonet let us improve it. What way can bring the slaves redemption more speedily. Wouldn’t it be rich to march an army through the slave holding states & roll up a black cloud that should spread dismay & terror to the ranks of the oppressors?”

>-Source: Doctor Charles Robinson, in a letter to Eli Thayer, 1855


**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how pro-slavery and antislavery advocates could not compromise over the extension of slavery
how the federal government increased its efforts to keep the United States from civil war
how abolitionists and enslaved people worked together to organize slave revolts in the South
how abolitionists were less committed than defenders of slavery to their cause

assessment_items.question_content:
>“I believe this government cannot endure, permanently half *slave* and half *free.* I do not expect the Union to be *dissolved* -- I do not expect the house to *fall* -- but I *do* expect it will cease to be divided. It will become *all* one thing or *all* the other.

> “Either the *opponents* of slavery, will arrest the further spread of it, and place it where the public mind shall rest in the belief that it is in the course of ultimate extinction; or its *advocates* will push it forward, till it shall become alike lawful in *all* the States, *old* as well as *new* -- *North* as well as *South*. . . . Let any one who doubts, carefully contemplate that now almost complete legal combination -- piece of *machinery* so to speak -- compounded of the Nebraska doctrine, and the Dred Scott decision.”

> -Source: Abraham Lincoln, “House Divided” speech, 1858


**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the renewed question of slavery’s expansion into territories north of the Missouri Compromise line
the emergence of nativist sentiment in national politics through the Know-Nothing Party
the secession of seven slave states in the period between Lincoln’s election and inauguration
the admission of Missouri and Maine into the Union to balance power between free and slave states

assessment_items.question_content:
>“I believe this government cannot endure, permanently half *slave* and half *free.* I do not expect the Union to be *dissolved* -- I do not expect the house to *fall* -- but I *do* expect it will cease to be divided. It will become *all* one thing or *all* the other.

> “Either the *opponents* of slavery, will arrest the further spread of it, and place it where the public mind shall rest in the belief that it is in the course of ultimate extinction; or its *advocates* will push it forward, till it shall become alike lawful in *all* the States, *old* as well as *new* -- *North* as well as *South*. . . . Let any one who doubts, carefully contemplate that now almost complete legal combination -- piece of *machinery* so to speak -- compounded of the Nebraska doctrine, and the Dred Scott decision.”

> -Source: Abraham Lincoln, “House Divided” speech, 1858


**The ideas expressed in the excerpt contributed most directly to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the emergence of sectional political parties
the drafting of the Lecompton Constitution
the founding of the abolitionist movement
the expansion of the Whig party

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a168544224fcd58960928a4a17eaddce.png)

>Political cartoon depicting Abraham Lincoln conducting a train poised to crash into a wagon labeled “Democratic Platform.” Currier & Ives, “Progressive democracy--prospect of a smash up.” 

>-Source: [Library of Congress,](https://www.loc.gov/resource/pga.04984/) 1860

**The image most closely reflects which of the following developments in the political climate in the United States?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the split of the two major parties into sectional parties over the issue of slavery
the embrace of the American System by Democratic candidates
the divide between Whigs and Democrats over the policy of Indian Removal
the compromise between pro-slavery and anti-slavery politicians

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a168544224fcd58960928a4a17eaddce.png)

>Political cartoon depicting Abraham Lincoln conducting a train poised to crash into a wagon labeled “Democratic Platform.” Currier & Ives, “Progressive democracy--prospect of a smash up.” 

>-Source: [Library of Congress,](https://www.loc.gov/resource/pga.04984/) 1860

**Phenomena like that shown in the image contributed most directly to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the end of the Second Party System
the passage of the Kansas-Nebraska Act
the ruling in the Dred Scott case
the opposition to slavery’s expansion

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Sir, the tendency of this bill is to stimulate the formation of a sectional party organization. And, as I said in my speech on the passage of the Senate bill, I regard that as the last and most fatal evil which can befall this country, except the dissolution of the Union; and that last and greatest calamity to the country, the success of such a movement would infallibly bring about.”

>-Source: John Bell, Speech on the Nebraska and Kansas Bill, 1854

**The remarks in the excerpt were most likely given in response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a legislative attempt to solve the issue of slavery in the territories
an amendment that extended citizenship to all free black people
a law that made it a crime to help people who escaped enslavement
an attempt to ban weapons in the territories to reduce violent conflict

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Sir, the tendency of this bill is to stimulate the formation of a sectional party organization. And, as I said in my speech on the passage of the Senate bill, I regard that as the last and most fatal evil which can befall this country, except the dissolution of the Union; and that last and greatest calamity to the country, the success of such a movement would infallibly bring about.”

>-Source: John Bell, Speech on the Nebraska and Kansas Bill, 1854

**The warnings expressed in the excerpt most directly foreshadowed which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the formation of the Republican Party
the rise of the Free-Soil Party
the passage of the Kansas-Nebraska Act
the Supreme Court decision in *Dred Scott v. Sandford*
